coredata.dc:title: Improving the oxygen evolution performance of nickel phosphide nanoparticles with satellite nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Wugang
Huang, Long
coredata.dc:description: A simple wet chemical method was used to deposit nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NC QDs) as satellites on the surface of nickel phosphide (Ni2P) nanoparticles. The structure of the NC QDs/Ni2P composite was confirmed by TEM, XRD and XPS. The electrocatalytic activity of the NC QDs/Ni2P composite for OER in alkaline solution was evaluated by RDE measurements. The nanocomposite fabricated as such can significantly enhance the electrocatalytic properties of Ni2P nanoparticles in the oxygen evolution reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nickel phosphide
Oxygen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of NC QDs (a), Ni2P nanoparticles (b), and NC QDs/Ni2P composite particles (c). XRD spectra of NC QDs, the as-prepared Ni2P nanoparticles and the NC QDs/Ni2P composite (d).
EDX elemental maps from a typical NC QDs/Ni2P sample shown in the center.

OER performance in 0.1M KOH. (a) CVs of NC QDs/Ni2P composite nanoparticles, Ni2P nanoparticles and 20wt% NC QDs/C. (b) LSV of Ni2P nanoparticles, 20wt% RuO2/C, NC QDs/Ni2P composite nanoparticles, and 20wt% NC QDs/C. (c) Tafel plots derived from (b). (d) The 1st and the 200th cycle LSV curves of NC QDs/Ni2P and 20wt% RuO2/C at 1600rpm. The 200 cycles of CV were scanned at a higher speed of 100mVs−1. The inset: Chronoamperograms (Current-time (i-t) curves) of NC QDs/Ni2P composite particles on carbon paper at 10mAg−1 (without rotation) .
XPS survey spectrum of as-prepared NC QD/Ni2P (a), high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p (b), and P 2p (c).

head.ce:title: Improving the oxygen evolution performance of nickel phosphide nanoparticles with satellite nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, National University of Singapore, 10 Kent Ridge Crescent, 119260, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Engineering, Xidian University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple wet chemical method was used to deposit nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NC QDs) as satellites on the surface of nickel phosphide (Ni2P) nanoparticles. The nanocomposite fabricated as such can significantly enhance the electrocatalytic properties of Ni2P nanoparticles in the oxygen evolution reaction.

A simple wet chemical method was used to deposit nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NC QDs) as satellites on the surface of nickel phosphide (Ni2P) nanoparticles. The structure of the NC QDs/Ni2P composite was confirmed by TEM, XRD and XPS. The electrocatalytic activity of the NC QDs/Ni2P composite for OER in alkaline solution was evaluated by RDE measurements. The nanocomposite fabricated as such can significantly enhance the electrocatalytic properties of Ni2P nanoparticles in the oxygen evolution reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is the basis of water splitting applications and is also central to the operation of rechargeable metal-air batteries. The OER is kinetically sluggish under most operating conditions and can only be accelerated in the presence of electrocatalysts [1–3]. Precious metals and their oxides are known for their good OER activities but their scarcity and prohibitively high cost are limiting their suitability for large scale water-splitting and metal-air batteries. Nickel phosphide (Ni2P) nanomaterials, which are used for the efficient catalysis of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [4–11], were also found to be OER active by the Hu group [6]. However, the relatively low conductivity of the as-prepared Ni2P nanoparticles and their propensity for aggregation [12] are limiting the charge transfer and the number of active sites in the OER process. Combining the Ni2P nanoparticles with conducting “spacer” nanoparticles may offer a solution; especially if the latter are rendered as satellites on the former to make use of the multiplicity of point contacts to support electron conduction without a significant decrease of the Ni2P catalytically active surface area. Carbon quantum dots (C QDs are therefore used in this study in conjunction with the Ni2P nanoparticles. Nitrogen doping can also be used to induce charge/spin redistribution to further improve their electron transport properties [13,14]. Hence the conjugation of nitrogen-doped C QDs (NC QDs) with Ni2P nanoparticles may provide a synergy of functions and uses the Ni2P nanoparticles more effectively for OER.

The electrochemical OER performance was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry. A glassy carbon (GC) working electrode with 0.1mgcm−2 loading of the catalyst was scanned from 1.2 to 1.8V at 5mVs−1 in 0.1M KOH. The working electrode was rotated at 1600rpm to remove the oxygen bubbles. 30 scans were used to condition the catalyst (to a constant population of surface nickel oxides/hydroxides) [6] to yield a stable voltammetric response. In the voltammograms shown in Fig. 3 a, an anodic peak at ∼1.45V and a cathodic peak at ∼1.38V were found for both Ni2P and NC QDs/Ni2P catalysts in the pre-OER potential region. The locations of these peaks suggest their association with the Ni3+/Ni2+ redox reaction [16]. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) responses of these catalysts were also benchmarked against a 20wt% RuO2 on carbon (Vulcan XC-72) (RuO2/C). The NC QDs/Ni2P composite excelled in these comparisons (Fig. 3b) Above the onset potential, its current density increased more rapidly with potential than 20wt% NC QDs/C or the Ni2P nanoparticles, indicating that the deposition of NC QDs on the Ni2P surface has produced a synergistic catalytic outcome for OER. In addition, on a stationary GC electrode, oxygen effervescence was most apparent on the electrode with the NC QDs/Ni2Pcatalyst (Fig. S2, ESI†), yet another indication of the effectiveness of this catalyst for OER.

The Tafel slope of 49.7mV/dec for the NC QDs/Ni2P composite in Fig. 3c is about half of that of the pristine Ni2P nanoparticles (88.9mV/dec), and accounts for most of the facile OER kinetic of the former. The stability of the NC QDs/Ni2P catalyst was also compared with that of 20wt% RuO2/C in Fig. 3d. Current density decreased substantially after 200 cycles for the latter. On the contrary the 200th cycle LSV curve of the NC QDs/Ni2P catalyst was almost identical to the 1st cycle LSV. The current-time (i-t) response of the NC QDs/Ni2P composite on carbon paper measured at current density of 10 mA g−1 (shown in the inset of Fig. 3d) further substantiated the stability of this catalyst. NC QDs/Ni2P composites with different NC QD contents were prepared, with measurements of their electrocatalytic performance in OER (Fig. S3, ESI†) The results showed that the composite containing 10wt% of NC QDs has the highest OER activity. A lower NC QD content might not disperse the Ni2P nanoparticles as effectively and the conductivity improvement could also be inadequate. On the other hand, too high a NC QD content could significantly reduce the number of catalytically active Ni2P sites per gram of composite.

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4 a) confirms the presence of Ni, P, C, O and N elements on the NC QDs/Ni2P surface. The Ni 2p peaks in the high resolution spectra in Fig. 4b at ∼852.8eV (2p3/2) and ∼870.1eV (2p1/2) can be attributed to Niδ+ in Ni2P [5]. We have also measured the Ni oxidation state in the NC QDs/Ni2P composite after OER catalysis for 30,000s. The Ni 3p spectrum (Fig. S4, ESI†) at 68.0, 70.2 and 75.0eV assignable to Ni 3p3/2, Ni 3p1/2 and the satellite peak of Ni 3p3/2 respectively. The intense Ni 3p3/2 peak at 68.0eV suggests the presence of a large amount of nickel oxide (NiOx) species [17] on the catalyst surface after OER catalysis. When the NC QDs/Ni2P catalyst after OER catalysis was examined by HRTEM, a lattice spacing of 0.209nm corresponding well with NiOx species was also found in the HRTEM image (Fig. S5, ESI†). The high resolution P 2p spectra of the NC QDs/Ni2P catalyst before OER in Fig. 4c show two peaks at 129.6eV and 131.5eV assignable to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 respectively. After OER catalysis only a broad peak at 133.67eV corresponding to totally oxidized P was present on the surface of the used catalyst. The O 1s spectra were also examined to gain additional insights into the OER catalysis. A NiOC bond at 530.2eV [18] was found in the high resolution O 1s spectrum of the as-prepared catalyst sample (Fig. S6a, ESI†). The other two peaks at 531.2 and 533.1eV could be assigned to CO/NiO and COC/COH respectively [18]. After OER catalysis, a hump (Fig. S6b, ESI†) appeared at ∼535.5eV which corresponds well with singly-bonded oxygen [19]. This is another indication of the surface oxidation of NC QDs/Ni2P and is consistent with the P 2p and Ni 2p spectra analyses.

The following hypothesis may therefore be proposed to account for the high electrocatalytic activity and excellent stability of the NC QDs/Ni2P catalyst. First and foremost, the small particle size and large specific volume of NC QDs and Ni2P nanoparticles can increase the availability and the catalytic activity of the OER active sites. Enhanced catalytic activity may also be due to the presence of nickel phosphate and oxide/hydroxide species on the Ni2P surface after voltammetric activation. Secondly, the presence of NC QDs in the composite maintains the Ni2P nanoparticles in a more dispersed state and also decreases the external electron transport resistance. The strong interaction between NC QDs and Ni2P nanoparticles as shown by their molecular linkage is expected to facilitate charge transfer and increase the durability of the NC QDs/Ni2P catalyst in OER.

We have produced a NC QDs/Ni2P composite catalyst by a simple wet chemical method. It was found that the NC QDs/Ni2P composite catalyst easily surpasses pristine NC QDs or Ni2P nanoparticles in OER performance. The Tafel slope for OER is a low 49.7mV/dec, and the catalyst is stable for at least 30,000s. This good OER performance may be attributed to the synergy in integrating NC QDs with Ni2P nanoparticles.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: W. Liao acknowledges Prof. Jim Yang Lee for his deliberate revision of this manuscript, and the helpful discussions with Prof. Xianmao Lu, Dr. Chunxian Guo, Dr. Shiliu Yang and Dr Yi Zhan from the department of chemical and biomolecular engineering, National University of Singapore.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.07.127.
